When this signature alerts on an executable, it is not an indication of malicious behavior. However, if seen firing in other file types, deeper investigation may be warranted.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Dot net compiler compiles file from suspicious location
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Dot net compiler compiles file from suspicious location
Detects execution of "csc.exe" to compile .NET code. Attackers often leverage this to compile code on the fly and use it in other stages.

When C# is compiled dynamically, a .cmdline file will be created as a part of the process.
Certain processes are not typically observed compiling C# code, but can do so without touching disk.
This can be used to unpack a payload for execution

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The sample is a malicious downloader and dropper. It exhibits several high-risk behaviors: it copies itself to the Windows Temp directory as 'budha.exe' and executes the copy hiddenly using ShellExecuteW with SW_HIDE. It dynamically resolves 'RtlDecompressBuffer' from ntdll.dll to decompress an embedded configuration. The malware then initiates network connections using the WinInet API with a hardcoded User-Agent 'Updates downloader' to fetch an encrypted payload. The downloaded data is decrypted via XOR, decompressed, verified for an 'MZ' header, and written to disk as 'kilf1.exe' before being executed silently. Relevant IOCs include the filenames 'budha.exe' and 'kilf1.exe', the 'Updates downloader' User-Agent, and the use of the 'RtlDecompressBuffer' API for obfuscated payload handling.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The analyzed binary is a legitimate command-line utility used for monitoring and displaying statistics for ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode) network interfaces, likely a version of the Windows system tool 'atmstat.exe'. Its primary behavior involves opening a handle to a network device via CreateFileA and using DeviceIoControl with specific ATM-related IOCTLs (e.g., 0x2101e0 for interface lists, 0x2101e4 for addresses, and 0x2101e8 for connections) to retrieve telemetry. The code contains strings and logic related to network parameters such as VPI/VCI (Virtual Path Identifier/Virtual Channel Identifier) and service categories. No evidence of malicious intent, persistence, or unauthorized data exfiltration was found.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs
